title: Entryway Cubby Bench and Hook Shelf Set
summary: A matched pair — a solid-board cubby bench with a divided seat base, and a wall shelf with angled-corner sides and hooks — built as two separate 1x-board units, not one plywood carcass.
description:
The bench and the wall shelf are two genuinely separate builds meant to hang and sit together, each made from solid 1x12 or 1x8 boards rather than a plywood carcass — a different construction from this catalog's poplar-over-plywood entryway bench. On the bench, attach the main boards with either screws or pocket holes and glue, then measure and mark the divider positions before attaching them, since the dividers set the cubby spacing for whatever's stored underneath.

**The bench is intentionally built narrow with 1x12s, and the plan calls this out directly** — substitute a wider project panel for every 1x12 if you want a deeper seat, and the whole design scales with it. If the front footer trim is fighting you where it meets the bottom board, a scrap 1x4 block flat-nailed to the underside in just the center area gives it something to bite into.

On the shelf, **cut the sides off at an angle rather than leaving them square** — this keeps a sharp corner from sitting at eye level where someone could catch it walking past. Attach the double hooks to the hook board before hanging, then mount the whole shelf by locating a wall stud and screwing through the hook board into it — the mounting hole can be hidden afterward with wood filler and matching paint or stain.

materials.name: Self-tapping wood screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Cut off at an angle to avoid a sharp corner at eye level.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut the bench parts from the 1x12s (actual size 3/4" x 11 1/4"): one bottom at 46 1/2", two dividers at 13 1/4", two sides at 17 1/4", and one seat at 48". Take the bottom, both sides and one divider off the 8 ft board and the 48" seat plus the second divider off the 6 ft board — do NOT follow the cut list's material column here, which puts the seat and both sides on the 6 ft board and asks for 82 1/2" of parts from a 72" board. From the two 8 ft 1x4s cut one top back trim at 48", two top side trims at 12", two footer sides at 11 1/4" and one footer front at 49 1/2" (footer front, both side trims and one footer side on the first board; the back trim and the second footer side on the second). For the shelf, cut both 46 1/2" shelf/back boards from the 8 ft 1x8, and cut the two 16 1/2" sides, the 49 1/2" top front and both 8 1/2" dividers from the 10 ft 1x8 — the dividers will not fit on the 8 ft board alongside the two 46 1/2" pieces. Cut the 49 1/2" shelf top back from the 6 ft 1x2, and cut the two plywood backs from the 48" x 24" quarter-inch panel: 48" x 14" for the bench and 48" x 10" for the shelf, which together use the panel's full 24" width exactly. Square every cut and label each piece.

steps.title: Build the bench base
steps.body: The two 17 1/4" sides run the full height and everything else fits between them: the 46 1/2" bottom plus the two 3/4" sides makes 48" overall, which is exactly the length of the seat that lands on top. Stand the sides up, set the bottom between them, and fasten through the outside faces with 2" screws and glue, or with pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws driven from underneath so nothing shows. Then mark the two divider positions on the bottom board and square them up before fastening — the dividers set the cubby widths for whatever goes underneath, so measure them off one end and check both marks against each other. The 13 1/4" dividers are shorter than the full side height, which puts the bottom board about 3 1/4" up from the floor ends of the sides where the footer trim will wrap; check that before you fasten, because the dividers have to land on the bottom and reach the underside of the seat.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Attach the seat
steps.body: Nail the 48" top back trim along the back edge of the 48" seat with 1 1/4" brads and glue, keeping both ends flush — the two boards are the same length, so a flush end on one end proves the other. Set the seat down on the base with its ends flush to the outside faces of the sides and its back edge over the back of the carcass, then fasten it with 2" screws down into the sides and dividers, or with pocket holes drilled up through the sides so the seat face stays clean. Push down on the middle of the seat as you drive the screws over the dividers; a divider that is a hair short leaves a gap you can pull shut now and never can later.
materials: Self-tapping wood screws, 2", or pocket hole screws, 1-1/4"

steps.title: Add the bench trim
steps.body: The trim is all cut to fit, so hold each piece in place and mark it against the assembled bench rather than trusting the listed length. The 49 1/2" footer front laps the full 48" outside width plus the 3/4" thickness of a footer side at each end, so fit the two 11 1/4" footer sides first and let the front cover their end grain. The two 12" top side trims run under the ends of the seat to match the back trim. Glue and 1 1/4" brads hold all of it; nail into the sides and the bottom board, not into thin air, and fill the nail holes later with the wood filler.

steps.title: Attach the bench back
steps.body: Cut the bench back 48" x 14" from the quarter-inch plywood with a circular saw and keep the offcut — the shelf back at 48" x 10" comes out of the same 24"-wide panel and the two together use its full width, so a wandering cut costs you the second piece. Set the back on with its long edge flush to the outside width and fasten with 3/4" brads into the sides, the bottom and both dividers. A back nailed on square is what locks the whole carcass out of racking, so check a diagonal before the second edge goes down.

steps.title: Build the shelf
steps.body: The shelf is built like the bench: the two 16 1/2" sides run full height and the 46 1/2" boards sit between them, making the same 48" outside width. One 46 1/2" board is the hook board standing on edge at the back and the other is the horizontal shelf that lands on top of it — the side height works out exactly, with the 7 1/4" hook board plus the 3/4" shelf plus the 8 1/2" dividers filling the 16 1/2" side. Cut the top front corner off each side at an angle before assembly rather than leaving a square point at eye level, and cut both sides together or trace one from the other so the angles match. Glue and 1 1/4" brads through the sides into the ends of the shelf boards; then stand the two 8 1/2" dividers on the shelf, marked off one end so the bays come out even.

steps.title: Attach the shelf top
steps.body: The top is two boards: the 49 1/2" x 7 1/4" top front and the 49 1/2" x 1 1/2" top back, which together make a top about 8 3/4" deep over 7 1/4"-deep sides. Both are 49 1/2" against the shelf's 48" outside width, so center them and you get a 3/4" overhang at each end. Fasten with glue and 1 1/4" brads down into the side tops and the divider tops, keeping the back edge of the 1x2 flush with the back of the carcass so the whole unit still sits flat to the wall.

steps.title: Attach the shelf back and hooks
steps.body: Fasten the 48" x 10" plywood back on with glue and 3/4" brads into the sides, the shelf and both dividers, checking a diagonal first — same job as on the bench, squaring the unit permanently. Then lay out the three double hooks across the 46 1/2" hook board, marking them evenly off one end and all on one level line, and predrill before driving their screws so a 3/4" board does not split. Mount the hooks now, while the shelf is on a bench and you can reach both faces, rather than after it is on the wall.

steps.title: Finish and hang
steps.body: Fill the nail and screw holes, let the filler dry, then sand both pieces smooth and ease every edge — especially the front edge of the bench seat and the angled shelf corners. Prime and paint or stain and seal both units before hanging, while you can still lay them flat. To hang the shelf, find a wall stud, hold the unit at the height you want with a level on the top, and drive a long screw through the hook board into the stud; the hook board is the thickest thing on the back and the only part strong enough to carry loaded hooks. Fill and touch up that screw head afterward, and set the bench under it.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Entryway cubby bench and matching hook shelf
